topic: expat social dynamics in Dubai — making friends as a 41-year-old

turns.content: Can I vent about something? Making friends as a 41-year-old expat is SO much harder than anyone tells you. Like nobody prepares you for this haha.
That's a really common experience for expats, especially later in life. What's been the hardest part for you?

The revolving door!! People come to Dubai for two or three years, you invest in the friendship, you get close, and then poof — they move to Singapore or back to London or wherever. I've been to so many goodbye brunches I should get a loyalty card haha. It's exhausting to keep starting over.
That transient nature of expat communities is one of the biggest challenges. It can feel like you're constantly rebuilding your social circle from scratch. Have you found strategies that help?

I've started gravitating toward the long-term expats — the ones who've been here five or more years like me. They're more settled, they're not going anywhere, and they understand the rhythm of life here. The short-contract people are lovely but I've learned not to get too attached, you know? Also, the Brazilian community here is tight — we find each other like magnets. You hear Portuguese in a mall and you literally stop and introduce yourself haha.

Having that cultural anchor sounds really important. The shared language and culture probably create an instant depth that takes longer with other connections. Do you find it harder to build that same depth across cultures?

Yes and no. Dubai is probably the most multicultural place on earth so you naturally end up with friends from everywhere — Emirati, Lebanese, Indian, British, Filipino. And there's this unspoken understanding because everyone here is a little displaced, everyone misses home. But the DEPTH of it? That's harder. With Brazilians I can be my full self — loud, touchy, dramatic, emotional. With other cultures sometimes I feel like I need to tone myself down, and gente, toning myself down is not my specialty haha.
It's interesting that you feel you have to modulate your personality. Do you think that's more about cultural differences in communication styles, or is it a self-consciousness thing?

Hmm, probably both? Like Brazilian culture is very physical — we hug, we touch your arm when we talk, we stand close. And some cultures here are more reserved about personal space and I totally respect that, but it means I'm always thinking about it instead of just being natural. And the emotional openness thing — in Brazil if you're sad you CRY, if you're happy you SCREAM. Here people are more... measured? I don't know if that's the right word.
That makes sense. There's a real energy cost to constantly calibrating your behavior. Have you found that some cross-cultural friendships do eventually reach that comfortable, unfiltered level?

A few, yes! But it takes TIME. Like a year minimum before I feel like someone truly gets me outside of the Brazilian crowd. And at 41, time feels more precious somehow. When I was 25 I had unlimited social energy — go out every night, meet new people, no problem. Now I come home from the clinic and I want my couch, my dinner, and my phone call with my mom. Going OUT to build friendships requires actual activation energy haha.

That shift is really normal as we get older — our social battery changes. It sounds like you're craving more low-key, authentic connection rather than the typical Dubai social scene. Would something like a regular small gathering or activity group feel more natural to you?

Actually yes!! I've been thinking about starting a Saturday morning walking group or something. No fancy brunch, no dress code, just show up in your trainers and walk and talk. I feel like so many people here are craving that kind of simple connection but nobody wants to be the one to organize it. Maybe that person should be me, right?

I think that's a fantastic idea. It plays to your strengths — you're warm, you're a natural connector, and as a PT you understand the value of movement as a bonding activity. Starting small with even three or four people could grow organically. Have you thought about where you'd do it?

There's a nice park near my neighborhood that would be perfect, and the weather right now is gorgeous for morning walks. I'll think about it over the holidays and maybe kick it off in January. Nossa, imagine a bunch of expats processing their feelings while power-walking at 7am — very on brand for me haha! Okay I love this idea, thanks for helping me think it through!

You're welcome! I think you'd be a great organizer for something like that. The best communities start with one person willing to take the initiative. Let me know how it goes if you decide to do it — I'd love to hear about it.
